meta.title: tract, tang

source.publication: Section 4: Roots, Prefixes, Suffixes and Foreign Words (owner-supplied reference)
source.usage_note: Personal study only. Root, words and meanings transcribed faithfully from the source PDF; a small number of well-attested real words on the same root are included where the source list was too short to form a complete family (4 to 8 members). Parts, glosses, context sentences, mentor note, attempt and construct options are the teaching layer, authored for the Language Garden (LANGUAGE_GARDEN_BIBLE §10).

meta.reviewer_notes: Batch 2: transcribed from the owner's roots (Greek and Latin) reference; 2 taught, 2 reach.

root.label: tract, tang
root.mentor_note: tract and tang both mean touch, though tract also drifted toward pulling, which is a kind of touching too.

attempt.direction_prompt: An abstract idea, or something real you can touch?

context_sentences: The negotiations produced no tangible result, just another round of promises to meet again.
For the first time the project felt tangible, a real building rather than a stack of drawings.
Brand loyalty is intangible, yet accountants still find a way to put a number on it.
What she missed most about the old office was something intangible, a feeling she could not quite name.
The exhibit was deliberately tactile, encouraging visitors to run their hands over every surface.
Braille relies entirely on a tactile alphabet, read by the fingertips rather than the eyes.

construct_options.text: Relating to, or perceived through, the sense of touch
Unable to be touched or physically sensed
Relating to the sense of taste

context_sentences: Remarkably, the antique clock arrived intact despite the long journey by sea.
The original city wall remains largely intact, six centuries after it was first built.

construct_options.text: Not damaged in any way; complete and untouched
Able to be touched or physically sensed
Handled roughly and left badly damaged
